winner.label: The Winner
winner.philosophy: Smart, boring, consistent decisions made early.

log.action: Moved two months of expenses to a high-yield savings account.
log.thinking: If it sits in checking, it earns nothing.

log.action: Calculated monthly burn rate for the first time. Result: $2,100/month.
log.thinking: I need to know exactly where I stand.

log.action: Still employed. Kept 5-month emergency fund intact. Did not touch investments.
log.thinking: This is exactly what the emergency fund is for. I'm watching, not panicking.

log.action: Markets down 40%. Started setting aside $200/month for index funds at these prices.
log.thinking: This is the sale. Every dollar I put in now is buying more shares than it would have last year.

log.action: Markets hit bottom March 9. Increased index fund contributions to $400/month.
log.thinking: I don't know if this is the bottom but I'm buying anyway. DCA removes that burden.

log.action: Roth IRA maxed for the second year. 401(k) up to match threshold.
log.thinking: The math on compound interest is starting to show. Three years of consistent investing.

log.action: Net worth crossed $90k. Increased 401(k) contribution above match for first time.
log.thinking: The portfolio is doing most of the work now. My job is not to interfere.

log.action: Longest bull market in history. Did not liquidate. Did not change strategy.
log.thinking: Everyone is asking me about crypto. I said no. My boring index funds are up 400% since 2009.

log.action: COVID crash begins. Did not sell. Added $1,000 lump sum at the low.
log.thinking: I've been through 2008. I know what this looks like. I'm buying, not selling.

log.action: Inflation at 8%. Moved idle cash to I-bonds and HYSA at 4%+. Still investing monthly.
log.thinking: High rates finally make saving cash worthwhile again. Still not changing the portfolio.

log.action: 18 years of boring decisions. Never touched the emergency fund except once.
log.thinking: The simulation is over. The real portfolio is not.

log.action: Saw the Fed cuts. Did not change where cash sat.
log.thinking: Headlines are noisy.

log.action: Tracked account balances weekly but avoided big moves.
log.thinking: Steady as it goes.

log.action: Still employed. Pulled back on spending slightly. Did not invest the difference.
log.thinking: Better to hold cash until this settles down.

log.action: Missed the opportunity to invest at market lows. Was waiting for clarity.
log.thinking: It still feels too uncertain. I'll wait until things look more stable.

log.action: Missed the market bottom entirely. Started a small savings account.
log.thinking: At least I didn't lose anything.

log.action: Contributing 3% to 401(k) — just enough to get partial match.
log.thinking: I keep meaning to increase it.

log.action: Bull market running. Portfolio growing, mostly from employer contributions.
log.thinking: This is going well enough.

log.action: Never increased contributions above 3%. Lifestyle crept up with raises.
log.thinking: The money always goes somewhere.

log.action: Sold 15% of 401(k) during the crash. Bought back in 4 months later at higher prices.
log.thinking: I couldn't watch it fall any further.

log.action: Inflation squeezed the budget. Put some expenses on credit card for the first time.
log.thinking: Rates are up, prices are up. Something had to give.

log.action: Stable. Never in crisis. Never compounding strongly.
log.thinking: Not bad. Could have been worse. Could have been much better.

unaware.label: The Unaware
unaware.philosophy: Did not know the rules. Most common outcome.

log.action: Did not see the news about rates.
log.thinking: Things feel normal.

log.action: Paid rent from checking. Did not compute runway.
log.thinking: Things will pick up soon.

log.action: Laid off in October. No emergency fund. First credit card charge to cover rent.
log.thinking: I'll find something in a few weeks.

log.action: Two months without income. Living on credit. UI benefits finally started.
log.thinking: I had no idea it would take this long.

log.action: Found part-time work. Minimum payments on credit card. Not investing.
log.thinking: Just trying to get back to zero.

log.action: Slowly paying down credit card. No retirement savings. Still rebuilding.
log.thinking: Almost back to zero. Took three years.

log.action: Credit card almost paid. Started 401(k) at 1% — no employer match captured.
log.thinking: I should have started this years ago.

log.action: Credit card debt crept back up. Only saving 1% to 401(k).
log.thinking: The rules feel designed for people who already have money.

log.action: Panic-sold 401(k) at the COVID bottom. Lost 18% permanently to taxes and penalties.
log.thinking: I couldn't afford to lose any more. I needed the cash.

log.action: Inflation hit hard. Minimum wage lagged. Debt growing again.
log.thinking: I don't understand why everything got so expensive.

log.action: 18 years from the same start. Not in crisis. Not compounding.
log.thinking: Nobody taught me any of this.

gambler_lost.label: The Gambler Who Lost
gambler_lost.philosophy: Knew everything. Acted on it badly.

log.action: Increased short exposure to financials.
log.thinking: The stress is tradable.

log.action: Bear Stearns short paid. Up 70% on the position. Rolled profits into new shorts.
log.thinking: Volatility is the product. I see what others don't.

log.action: Covered shorts at Lehman collapse — but too early. Left $8k on the table.
log.thinking: I knew it would fall. I just didn't hold long enough.

log.action: Went long financials at what I thought was the bottom. Down 30% in three weeks.
log.thinking: This was supposed to be the recovery trade.

log.action: Averaging down on a bad position. Using credit card to avoid liquidating.
log.thinking: It has to recover eventually.

log.action: Position eventually recovered. Paid off some debt. Now in gold. Waiting for 'the crash'.
log.thinking: Fiat can't last. I just need to be early enough.

log.action: Gold thesis failed. Sold at a loss after 2 years. Back in cash, looking for the next trade.
log.thinking: The trade was right. The timing was wrong.

log.action: Bought bitcoin near $18k in 2017. Held through 80% decline. Still holding.
log.thinking: I was early once. I can be early again.

log.action: Used margin to buy equities before COVID crash. Margin call wiped the account.
log.thinking: I did not expect a pandemic.

log.action: Borrowed to buy crypto in 2021 bull run. FTX collapse took 60% of what remained.
log.thinking: The thesis was right. The counterparty risk was not on my radar.

log.action: 18 years of making the right call at the wrong time.
log.thinking: I understood every concept. I mismanaged every position.

gambler_won.label: The Gambler Who Won
gambler_won.philosophy: Same knowledge as the loser. Better luck.

log.action: Increased short exposure to financials.
log.thinking: The stress is tradable.

log.action: Bear Stearns short paid. Up 70% on the position.
log.thinking: Volatility is the product. Staying in.

log.action: Held Lehman short through the full collapse. Closed at the bottom.
log.thinking: Held the same trade as before, but longer this time.

log.action: Bought SPY and individual bank stocks at the lows. Staying liquid otherwise.
log.thinking: Every crash ends. This one is historic.

log.action: Bought more at the exact March 9 bottom — pure luck of timing.
log.thinking: I thought it was the bottom. For once, it actually was.

log.action: Held equity positions through the recovery. Maxed Roth IRA. Portfolio compounding.
log.thinking: The short bets gave me the capital. The index funds are doing the rest.

log.action: Sold half the individual stocks. Moved to index funds. Kept one concentrated bet.
log.thinking: I was lucky. Luck doesn't repeat. Index funds do.

log.action: Bought bitcoin at $600 in 2015. Sold 70% near $18k in 2017.
log.thinking: Same trade, different exit than the loser.

log.action: COVID crash. Did not sell. Added lump sum at the bottom.
log.thinking: Portfolio is large enough that volatility no longer feels personal.

log.action: Inflation year. Moved idle cash to I-bonds and HYSA. Continued investing monthly.
log.thinking: High rates are the first time cash has actually competed with equities.

log.action: 18 years from the same $7,000 starting point.
log.thinking: The knowledge was the same as the other gambler. The luck was different. That's the whole lesson.
